title: Entrepreneur / Startup Founder Signals
summary: Founders operate where product, market, capital, hiring, and timing collide. The signal is about what people are actually paying for, what is becoming possible, and which teams can move faster than the market expects.

items.name: AI-native companies are changing startup economics
items.summary: Small teams can now produce more output, faster, with fewer people than before.
items.whyItMatters: The cost structure of starting a company is changing.

items.name: Distribution is often the moat
items.summary: Founders increasingly win by controlling distribution, not just building features.
items.whyItMatters: Getting attention and access can matter more than the raw product idea.

items.name: Capital discipline is back
items.summary: Many teams are moving from growth-at-all-costs to clearer paths to profitability.
items.whyItMatters: The market is rewarding sustainable companies more than hype-first stories.

items.name: Y Combinator
items.summary: A defining institution for startup formation and founder thinking.
items.whyItMatters: It sets the rhythm for how many new companies are imagined and funded.

items.name: Stripe
items.summary: A reference point for product-led infrastructure and founder execution.
items.whyItMatters: It shows what a durable platform company can look like.

items.name: Atlassian
items.summary: A useful model for product discipline and scaled software business building.
items.whyItMatters: It is a strong example of Australian software ambition at scale.

items.name: Startmate
items.summary: A strong Australian founder and startup ecosystem entry point.
items.whyItMatters: It is a key gateway into the local startup network.

items.name: Techstars
items.summary: A major global accelerator and founder support network.
items.whyItMatters: It is one of the recurring launch pads for new startups.

items.name: Universities and incubators
items.summary: Still a major source of technical talent, ideas, and company formation.
items.whyItMatters: Most startups still depend on the institutional pipeline around them.

items.name: Brian Chesky
items.summary: A strong example of product, brand, and marketplace thinking.
items.whyItMatters: He represents founder-led product clarity at scale.

items.name: Melanie Perkins
items.summary: A major Australian founder and execution benchmark.
items.whyItMatters: She is one of the clearest local signals for ambitious company building.

items.name: Reid Hoffman
items.summary: A visible thinker on networks, companies, and scaling.
items.whyItMatters: He is a useful public voice on how startups actually grow.

items.name: VC-backed growth versus bootstrapping
items.summary: Founders still argue about speed, dilution, and independence.
items.whyItMatters: The funding model changes the company you end up building.

items.name: What is the moat in an AI world?
items.summary: The answer may be product, distribution, data, or trust rather than raw model access.
items.whyItMatters: Founders need to understand where defensibility really lives.

items.name: How much should founders optimise for revenue early?
items.summary: The market is divided between growth-first and profitability-first strategies.
items.whyItMatters: Cash discipline can extend runway and change survival odds.
